era_context: The late 11th century was a period of significant political consolidation and religious reform across Eurasia. The Seljuk Empire dominated the Middle East; the Norman kingdoms controlled England and southern Italy; the Reconquista was advancing in Iberia; the Song Dynasty ruled a prosperous but militarily pressured China; and the Byzantine Empire was recovering under the Komnenos dynasty. This era witnessed the height of feudalism in Western Europe, the flourishing of Islamic civilization, and the early stirrings of the Crusade movement.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asian steppe (known primarily through Chinese and Islamic sources)

events.title: Henry IV Besieges Rome; Gregory VII Flees
events.description: In 1084, Holy Roman Emperor Henry IV besieged Rome in an attempt to force Pope Gregory VII to recognize his authority over ecclesiastical appointments. The siege was part of the ongoing Investiture Controversy, in which Henry and Gregory were locked in a struggle over the right to invest bishops. During the siege, the Norman knight Robert Guiscard came to Gregory's aid, but his forces sacked the city in the process. Gregory was forced to flee Rome with Guiscard's army, effectively ending his papacy in Rome. This event demonstrated the vulnerability of papal authority and the complex politics of medieval Italy.

sources.name: Saxon Anonymous, Life of Henry IV
William of Apulia, Gesta Roberti Wiscardi

events.certainty_note: Henry IV's siege of Rome in 1084 and Gregory VII's flight are well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Seljuk Empire: Malik Shah Consolidates Control
events.description: Sultan Malik Shah (r. 1072–1092) continued to consolidate Seljuk control over vast territories in 1084. The empire was at the height of its power, controlling regions from Anatolia to Khorasan. Malik Shah's reign was characterized by administrative efficiency, military strength, and cultural patronage. The Nizamiyya madrasas, founded by his vizier Nizam al-Mulk, were flourishing as centers of Islamic learning and theology. The Seljuk Empire was one of the most powerful and culturally advanced states in the medieval world.

sources.name: Seljuk administrative records and later Islamic chronicles

events.certainty_note: Malik Shah's reign and the Seljuk Empire's territorial extent are well-attested in Islamic sources.

events.title: Song Dynasty: Administrative Reforms and Military Pressures
events.description: In 1084, the Song Dynasty under Emperor Shenzong (r. 1067–1085) was in the final year of his reign. The empire was implementing administrative reforms, including the New Policies (Xin Fa) of Wang Anshi, which aimed to strengthen the state's military and fiscal capacity. However, the Song faced persistent military pressure from the Jurchen Jin Dynasty in the north and the Western Xia in the northwest. The Song Dynasty was prosperous and culturally advanced, but military weakness remained a chronic problem. Shenzong's reign would end in 1085 with the succession of his young son Emperor Zhezong.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Song official records and administrative documents

events.certainty_note: Song Dynasty political and administrative history is well-documented in Chinese chronicles and official records.

events.title: Byzantine Empire: Alexios I Komnenos Consolidates Power
events.description: Emperor Alexios I Komnenos (r. 1081–1118) continued his consolidation of Byzantine power in 1084. Having taken power after the catastrophic defeat at Manzikert (1071), Alexios implemented military reforms, reorganized the provincial administration, and worked to restore the empire's financial stability. In 1084, he was consolidating control over the Balkans and preparing for future conflicts with the Normans and the Seljuks. His reign marked the beginning of the Komnenos dynasty's restoration of Byzantine power and prestige.

sources.name: Byzantine administrative records and military treatises

events.certainty_note: Alexios I's reign and policies are well-documented in Byzantine sources.

events.title: Iberia: Christian Reconquista Advances; Toledo Siege Continues
events.description: In 1084, Alfonso VI of Castile and León was besieging the city of Toledo, one of the largest and most important cities in al-Andalus. The siege would culminate in Toledo's capture in May 1085. This campaign represented a major Christian advance in the Reconquista and would prompt the Almoravid intervention from North Africa. The siege of Toledo was a pivotal moment in the long struggle between Christian and Muslim powers in Iberia.
key_figures: Alfonso VI of Castile and León

sources.name: Chronicle of Alfonso VI; Estoria de España

events.certainty_note: The siege of Toledo in 1084 is attested in sources, though the exact timeline is sometimes unclear; the capture is definitively dated to May 1085.

events.title: Japan: Heian Period Court Politics and Cultural Flourishing
events.description: In 1084, Japan was in the Heian period, dominated by the Fujiwara regency system and characterized by significant cultural developments. The imperial court was a center of literary and artistic achievement, with the flourishing of Japanese literature, calligraphy, and painting. Emperor Shirakawa was ruling with Fujiwara regents, and the court was engaged in elaborate ceremonies and cultural patronage. However, the samurai warrior class was gradually gaining power, and the Fujiwara regency system was beginning to decline. This period saw the composition of important literary works and the development of distinctly Japanese cultural forms.
events.certainty_note: Japanese court politics and cultural developments are well-documented in Japanese historical records.

events.title: Norman Kingdom of Sicily: Consolidation of Conquest
events.description: In 1084, the Norman conquest of southern Italy and Sicily was nearing completion. Robert Guiscard and his successors had been systematically conquering Byzantine and Muslim territories in the region since the 1050s. By 1084, Norman control was firmly established over most of southern Italy and Sicily, creating a powerful Norman kingdom that rivaled the Byzantine Empire and the Papal States. The Normans were consolidating their administrative control and integrating the diverse populations of their new territories.

sources.name: William of Apulia, Gesta Roberti Wiscardi
Amatus of Montecassino, History of the Normans

events.certainty_note: Norman consolidation of southern Italy and Sicily by 1084 is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Henry IV's siege of Rome is sometimes overstated as a complete victory; while he forced Gregory VII to flee, his position remained contested, and the Investiture Controversy continued for decades. The sack of Rome by Guiscard's forces is sometimes attributed entirely to Henry IV's siege, but it was actually Guiscard's relief force that caused the destruction. The Seljuk Empire's stability under Malik Shah is sometimes presented as more complete than it actually was; regional governors exercised considerable autonomy. The Song Dynasty's military weakness is sometimes portrayed as a permanent condition, but the Song actually had significant military capabilities and won important victories.
historiographic_note: The year 1084 is well-documented in Western European sources, particularly regarding the Investiture Controversy and the siege of Rome. Islamic sources provide good coverage of Seljuk affairs. Chinese sources are rich for Song Dynasty history. Byzantine sources are adequate but often written from a court perspective. Japanese sources are good for court history. However, sources are heavily weighted toward elite political, military, and religious events; the lives of common people, women, and non-literate societies are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan African, Northern European, and Central Asian histories are poorly represented in surviving written sources for this year.

graph_edges.note: Henry IV's siege of Rome and Gregory VII's flight contributed to Gregory's death in 1085
The siege of Toledo in 1084 culminated in its capture in May 1085
Malik Shah's consolidation continued from 1084 to 1085
